examples.title: Find Middle Element Using Slow Fast Pointers
examples.description: Single pass algorithm to find middle of linked list
examples.code:
FUNCTION FindMiddle(head)
  IF head = NULL THEN
    RETURN NULL
  ENDIF
  
  slow = head
  fast = head
  
  WHILE fast != NULL AND fast.next != NULL DO
    slow = slow.next
    fast = fast.next.next
  ENDWHILE
  
  RETURN slow  // Middle element
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: Slow pointer moves one step, fast moves two steps. When fast reaches end, slow is at middle. For even-length lists, returns second middle.

examples.title: Find Nth Node From End
examples.description: Two pointer technique to find nth node from end in one pass
examples.code:
FUNCTION FindNthFromEnd(head, n)
  IF head = NULL OR n <= 0 THEN
    RETURN NULL
  ENDIF
  
  first = head
  second = head
  
  // Move first pointer n nodes ahead
  FOR i = 1 TO n DO
    IF first = NULL THEN
      RETURN NULL  // n exceeds length
    ENDIF
    first = first.next
  ENDFOR
  
  // Move both pointers together
  WHILE first != NULL DO
    second = second.next
    first = first.next
  ENDWHILE
  
  RETURN second
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: First pointer moves n nodes ahead, then both pointers move together. When first reaches end, second pointer is at nth position from end.

examples.title: Reverse Linked List Iteratively
examples.description: Iterative algorithm to reverse linked list links
examples.code:
FUNCTION ReverseList(head)
  prev = NULL
  current = head
  
  WHILE current != NULL DO
    nextNode = current.next  // Save next
    current.next = prev      // Reverse link
    prev = current            // Move prev forward
    current = nextNode        // Move current forward
  ENDWHILE
  
  RETURN prev  // New head
END FUNCTION
examples.explanation: Uses three pointers to reverse each link. prev starts NULL, current traverses list. Each node's next is changed to point to previous node.

examples.title: Detect Cycle Using Floyd's Algorithm
examples.explanation: hasCycle returns boolean if cycle exists. findCycleStart finds the node where cycle begins using the mathematical relationship that distance from head to cycle start equals distance from meeting point to cycle start.
